source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 16: Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall notify users in a clear manner about the situation of the algorithmic recommendation services they provide, and publicize the basic principles, purposes and motives, main operational mechanisms, etc., of the algorithmic recommendation services in a suitable manner.
gold_issue_tags: user notice; algorithmic transparency; service mechanism disclosure
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; users
gold_obligations: providers shall clearly notify users about the algorithmic recommendation services they provide; providers shall publicize basic principles, purposes and motives, and main operational mechanisms in a suitable manner
gold_support_spans: shall notify users in a clear manner about the situation of the algorithmic recommendation services they provide; publicize the basic principles, purposes and motives, main operational mechanisms, etc.; of the algorithmic recommendation services in a suitable manner
known_confounds: the passage does not define suitable manner; the passage does not specify timing, format, or enforcement consequences; the passage does not require revealing source code or full algorithm details
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: specific disclosure template; requirement to reveal source code or full algorithm; specific penalty for inadequate notice; obligation to obtain user consent
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 17: Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall provide users with a choice to not target their individual characteristics, or provide users with a convenient option to switch off algorithmic recommendation services. Where users choose to switch off algorithmic recommendation services, the algorithmic recommendation service provider shall immediately cease providing related services. Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall provide users with functions to choose or delete user tags used for algorithmic recommendation services aimed at their personal characteristics. Where algorithmic recommendation service providers use algorithms in a manner creating a major influence on users’ rights and interests, they shall give an explanation and bear related liability according to the law.
gold_issue_tags: non-targeted recommendation option; recommendation switch-off right; user tag controls; explanation for major rights impacts
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; users
gold_obligations: providers shall offer a choice not to target individual characteristics or a convenient switch-off option; providers shall immediately cease related services when users switch off algorithmic recommendation services; providers shall let users choose or delete user tags used for personal-characteristic recommendations; providers using algorithms in a way that creates major influence on users rights and interests shall explain and bear related liability according to law
gold_support_spans: shall provide users with a choice to not target their individual characteristics; provide users with a convenient option to switch off algorithmic recommendation services; shall immediately cease providing related services; shall provide users with functions to choose or delete user tags; they shall give an explanation and bear related liability according to the law
known_confounds: major influence on users’ rights and interests is not defined; the passage does not specify complaint process or remedies beyond explanation and related liability; individual characteristics and related services boundaries are not detailed
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: users have a right to monetary compensation from this passage alone; providers must delete all personal data; specific deadline other than immediately for ceasing related services; ban on all personalization
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 18: Where algorithmic recommendation service providers provide services to minors, they shall fulfill duties for the online protection of minors according to the law, and make it convenient for minors to obtain information beneficial to their physical and mental health, through developing models suited for use with minors, providing services suited to the specific characteristics of minors, etc. Algorithmic recommendation service providers may not push information toward minors that may incite the minor to imitate unsafe conduct, or acts violating social morals, or lead the minor towards harmful tendencies or may influence minors’ physical and mental health in other ways; and they may not use algorithmic recommendation services to lead minors to online addiction.
gold_issue_tags: minor protection; beneficial content access; harmful content restriction; online addiction prevention
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; minors
gold_obligations: when serving minors, providers shall fulfill online protection duties according to law; providers shall make it convenient for minors to obtain information beneficial to physical and mental health; providers may not push information to minors that may incite unsafe conduct, immoral conduct, harmful tendencies, or other physical and mental health impacts; providers may not use algorithmic recommendation services to lead minors to online addiction
gold_support_spans: Where algorithmic recommendation service providers provide services to minors; they shall fulfill duties for the online protection of minors according to the law; make it convenient for minors to obtain information beneficial to their physical and mental health; may not push information toward minors that may incite the minor to imitate unsafe conduct; they may not use algorithmic recommendation services to lead minors to online addiction
known_confounds: the passage references duties according to law without detailing them; criteria for harmful tendencies and physical and mental health impacts are not specified; age thresholds for minors are not stated
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: specific parental consent requirements; mandatory human review of all recommendations to minors; specific age verification technology; complete ban on minors using algorithmic recommendation services
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 19: Where algorithmic recommendation service providers provide services to the elderly, they shall uphold the rights the elderly enjoy according to the law, fully consider the elderly's requirement in going out, undergoing medical treatment, consumption, handling affairs, etc., provide smart services suited to the elderly according to relevant State provisions, launch monitoring, identification, and handling of information on telecommunications and online fraud, and make it convenient for the elderly to use algorithmic recommendation services securely. Article 20: Where algorithmic recommendation service providers provide work dispatch services to workers, they shall protect workers' lawful rights and interests such as obtaining labor remuneration, rest and vacation, etc., and establish and perfect algorithms related to platform sign-on and allocation, remuneration composition and payment, work time, rewards, etc.
gold_issue_tags: elderly user rights; elderly-suited smart services; telecom and online fraud protection; worker rights in dispatch services; platform labor algorithm governance
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; elderly users; workers
gold_obligations: when serving elderly users, providers shall uphold legally enjoyed rights and consider elderly needs in travel, medical treatment, consumption, and handling affairs; providers shall provide smart services suited to the elderly and make secure use convenient; providers shall monitor, identify, and handle telecommunications and online fraud information; providers of work dispatch services shall protect workers lawful rights and interests including remuneration, rest, and vacation; providers shall establish and perfect algorithms related to platform sign-on and allocation, remuneration, work time, and rewards
gold_support_spans: Where algorithmic recommendation service providers provide services to the elderly, they shall uphold the rights the elderly enjoy according to the law; fully consider the elderly's requirement in going out, undergoing medical treatment, consumption, handling affairs, etc.; provide smart services suited to the elderly according to relevant State provisions; launch monitoring, identification, and handling of information on telecommunications and online fraud; they shall protect workers' lawful rights and interests; establish and perfect algorithms related to platform sign-on and allocation, remuneration composition and payment, work time, rewards, etc.
known_confounds: the passage covers elderly services and worker dispatch services in the same segment; specific smart-service standards are not defined; worker protection details are examples and not a complete labor-law specification; fraud handling process and thresholds are not specified
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: specific wage rate or rest-hour entitlement; guarantee that all elderly users receive human assistance; specific anti-fraud detection technology; applies to non-dispatch employment outside covered algorithmic services
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 29: Related bodies and personnel participating in algorithmic recommendation service security assessment, supervision, and inspection shall maintain confidentiality of the personal private [information], personal information, and commercial secrets they learn when exercising their duties and responsibilities, they may not disclose, sell, or illegally provide it to other persons. Article 30: Where any organization or individual discovers acts violating these Provisions, they may file a complaint or report with cybersecurity and informatization departments and relevant departments. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall handle them timely and according to the law. Chapter V: Legal Liability

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 33: Where providers of algorithmic recommendation services with public opinion properties or social mobilization capabilities obtain filing through hiding relevant circumstances when reporting for filing, providing false materials, or other such improper means, the national and provincial, autonomous region, or municipal cybersecurity and informatization departments cancel filing according to the law, and issue a warning or a report of criticism; where circumstances are grave, they are to order provisional suspension of information updates, and impose a fine between 10,000 and 100,000 yuan. Where providers of algorithmic recommendation services with public opinion properties or social mobilization capabilities cease services without carrying out filing cancellation formalities according to the requirements of Article 24 Paragraph III of these Provisions, or they receive administrative punishments such as website closure orders, cancellation of relevant business permits, revocation of the business license, etc., because grave unlawful situations occurred, the national, provincial, autonomous region, and municipal cybersecurity and informatization departments are to impose filing cancellation. Chapter VI: Supplementary provisions
gold_issue_tags: filing fraud sanctions; filing cancellation; administrative penalties; public opinion or social mobilization services
gold_actors: providers of algorithmic recommendation services with public opinion properties or social mobilization capabilities; national cybersecurity and informatization departments; provincial cybersecurity and informatization departments; autonomous region cybersecurity and informatization departments; municipal cybersecurity and informatization departments
gold_obligations: cybersecurity and informatization departments cancel filing and issue a warning or report of criticism when covered providers obtain filing by hiding circumstances, false materials, or improper means; for grave circumstances, departments order provisional suspension of information updates and impose a fine between 10,000 and 100,000 yuan; departments impose filing cancellation when covered providers cease services without required filing cancellation formalities or receive serious administrative punishments because grave unlawful situations occurred
gold_support_spans: obtain filing through hiding relevant circumstances when reporting for filing, providing false materials, or other such improper means; cancel filing according to the law, and issue a warning or a report of criticism; order provisional suspension of information updates, and impose a fine between 10,000 and 100,000 yuan; cease services without carrying out filing cancellation formalities; the national, provincial, autonomous region, and municipal cybersecurity and informatization departments are to impose filing cancellation
known_confounds: the passage applies only to providers with public opinion properties or social mobilization capabilities; grave circumstances are not defined; Article 24 Paragraph III is referenced but not included; appeal or hearing rights are not detailed
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: penalties for all algorithmic recommendation providers; criminal liability; fine above 100,000 yuan from this passage; automatic website closure for every violation
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 6: Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall uphold mainstream value orientations, optimize algorithmic recommendation service mechanisms, vigorously disseminate positive energy, and advance the use of algorithms upwards and in the direction of good. Algorithmic recommendation service providers may not use algorithmic recommendation services to engage in activities harming national security and the social public interest, upsetting the economic order and social order, infringing the lawful rights and interests of other persons, and other such acts prohibited by laws and administrative regulations. They may not use algorithmic recommendation services to disseminate information prohibited by laws and administrative regulations, and shall take measures to prevent and curb the dissemination of harmful information.
gold_issue_tags: mainstream value orientation; prohibited algorithmic recommendation uses; harmful information prevention; unlawful content dissemination
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; users; other persons
gold_obligations: providers shall uphold mainstream value orientations, optimize recommendation mechanisms, disseminate positive energy, and advance algorithms upward and toward good; providers may not use algorithmic recommendation services for activities harming national security, public interest, economic or social order, or rights and interests of others; providers may not use algorithmic recommendation services to disseminate prohibited information; providers shall take measures to prevent and curb dissemination of harmful information
gold_support_spans: shall uphold mainstream value orientations; vigorously disseminate positive energy; may not use algorithmic recommendation services to engage in activities harming national security and the social public interest; upsetting the economic order and social order; infringing the lawful rights and interests of other persons; shall take measures to prevent and curb the dissemination of harmful information
known_confounds: the passage uses broad value and harm standards; specific measures to prevent harmful information are not specified; harmful information and prohibited information are not defined in this segment; enforcement penalties are not identified here
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: requirement to remove all controversial content; specific content moderation technology; application outside algorithmic recommendation services; specific government reporting duty in this article
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 7: Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall: fulfil their primary responsibility for algorithmic security, establish and complete management systems and technical measures for algorithmic mechanism examination and verification, technology ethics review, user registration, information dissemination examination and verification, security assessment and monitoring, security incident response and handling, data security and personal information protection, countering telecommunications and online fraud, etc.; formulate and disclose algorithmic recommendation service–related norms; and allocate specialized personnel and technical support suited to the scale of algorithmic recommendation services. Article 8: Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall regularly examine, verify, assess, and check algorithmic mechanisms, models, data, and application outcomes, etc., and may not set up algorithmic models that violate laws and regulations or ethics and morals, such as by leading users to addiction or excessive consumption.
gold_issue_tags: algorithmic security responsibility; governance systems and technical measures; technology ethics review; regular algorithm assessment; addiction and excessive consumption controls
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; users
gold_obligations: providers shall fulfill primary responsibility for algorithmic security; providers shall establish and complete management systems and technical measures for listed governance, security, data, personal information, and fraud controls; providers shall formulate and disclose service-related norms and allocate specialized personnel and technical support suited to service scale; providers shall regularly examine, verify, assess, and check mechanisms, models, data, and application outcomes; providers may not set up models violating laws, regulations, ethics, or morals, including leading users to addiction or excessive consumption
gold_support_spans: shall: fulfil their primary responsibility for algorithmic security; establish and complete management systems and technical measures; formulate and disclose algorithmic recommendation service–related norms; allocate specialized personnel and technical support suited to the scale; shall regularly examine, verify, assess, and check algorithmic mechanisms, models, data, and application outcomes; may not set up algorithmic models that violate laws and regulations or ethics and morals; such as by leading users to addiction or excessive consumption
known_confounds: specific cadence for regularly is not stated; appropriate personnel and support are scale-dependent but not quantified; ethics and morals standard is broad; addiction and excessive consumption are examples and may not exhaust the rule
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: required third-party audit; specific staffing numbers; specific security certification; complete ban on recommender models
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 9: Algorithmic recommendation service providers shall strengthen information security management; establish and complete feature databases to be used to identify unlawful and harmful information; and perfect database entry standards, norms, and processes. Where it is discovered that algorithmically generated or synthetic information has not been marked with an indicator, it shall be marked with an indicator, and only then may dissemination continue. Where unlawful information is discovered, transmission shall be ceased immediately, measures such as deletion adopted to handle it, information spread prevented, and relevant records preserved; and a report shall be made to the cybersecurity and informatization department and relevant departments. Where harmful information is discovered, it shall be dealt with according to online information content ecology management-related regulations.
gold_issue_tags: information security management; unlawful and harmful information database; synthetic information indicator; unlawful content takedown and reporting; records preservation
gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; cybersecurity and informatization department; relevant departments
gold_obligations: providers shall strengthen information security management; providers shall establish and complete feature databases to identify unlawful and harmful information and perfect database entry standards, norms, and processes; unmarked algorithmically generated or synthetic information must be marked before dissemination continues; when unlawful information is discovered, transmission shall cease immediately, deletion and other measures shall be adopted, spread prevented, records preserved, and a report made to competent departments; harmful information shall be handled according to online information content ecology management regulations
gold_support_spans: shall strengthen information security management; establish and complete feature databases to be used to identify unlawful and harmful information; perfect database entry standards, norms, and processes; Where it is discovered that algorithmically generated or synthetic information has not been marked with an indicator, it shall be marked with an indicator, and only then may dissemination continue; Where unlawful information is discovered, transmission shall be ceased immediately; measures such as deletion adopted to handle it; relevant records preserved; a report shall be made to the cybersecurity and informatization department and relevant departments; Where harmful information is discovered, it shall be dealt with according to online information content ecology management-related regulations

source_name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
passage: Article 12: Algorithmic recommendation service providers are encouraged to comprehensively use tactics such as content de-weighting, scattering interventions, etc., and optimize the transparency and understandability of search, ranking, selection, push notification, display, and other such norms, to avoid creating harmful influence on users, and prevent or reduce controversies or disputes. Article 13: Where algorithmic recommendation service providers provide Internet news information services, they shall obtain an Internet news information service permit according to the law; and standardize their deployment of Internet news information collection, editing and dissemination services, resharing services, and broadcast platform services. They may not generate or synthesize fake news information, and may not disseminate news information not published by work units in the State-determined scope.

gold_actors: algorithmic recommendation service providers; Internet news information service providers; work units in the State-determined scope

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: Article 86: Right to explanation of individual decision-making 1. Any affected person subject to a decision which is taken by the deployer on the basis of the output from a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III, with the exception of systems listed under point 2 thereof, and which produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects that person in a way that they consider to have an adverse impact on their health, safety or fundamental rights shall have the right to obtain from the deployer clear and meaningful explanations of the role of the AI system in the decision-making procedure and the main elements of the decision taken. 2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply to the use of AI systems for which exceptions from, or restrictions to, the obligation under that paragraph follow from Union or national law in compliance with Union law. 3. This Article shall apply only to the extent that the right referred to in paragraph 1 is not otherwise provided for under Union law.
gold_issue_tags: right to explanation; individual decision-making; high-risk AI deployer obligation; legal or similarly significant effects; exceptions and restrictions
gold_actors: affected persons; deployers
gold_obligations: affected persons subject to qualifying deployer decisions based on Annex III high-risk AI outputs have a right to clear and meaningful explanations from the deployer; the explanation must cover the role of the AI system in the decision-making procedure and the main elements of the decision taken; the right is limited by exceptions or restrictions under Union or national law and by overlap with rights otherwise provided under Union law
gold_support_spans: Any affected person subject to a decision which is taken by the deployer on the basis of the output from a high-risk AI system listed in Annex III; which produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects that person; shall have the right to obtain from the deployer clear and meaningful explanations; of the role of the AI system in the decision-making procedure and the main elements of the decision taken; Paragraph 1 shall not apply to the use of AI systems for which exceptions from, or restrictions to, the obligation under that paragraph follow from Union or national law; shall apply only to the extent that the right referred to in paragraph 1 is not otherwise provided for under Union law
known_confounds: the passage excludes systems listed under point 2 of Annex III; the decision must be taken by the deployer on the basis of the AI output; the effect must be legal or similarly significant and considered adverse by the affected person; Union or national law may create exceptions or restrictions; procedure, deadlines, and remedies are not detailed
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: universal right to explanation for all AI systems; right to source code or model weights; right to contest or reverse the decision; application where Union law already provides the same right
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: CHAPTER I: GENERAL PROVISIONS Article 1: Subject matter 1. The purpose of this Regulation is to improve the functioning of the internal market and promote the uptake of human-centric and trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI), while ensuring a high level of protection of health, safety, fundamental rights enshrined in the Charter, including democracy, the rule of law and environmental protection, against the harmful effects of AI systems in the Union and supporting innovation. 2. This Regulation lays down: (a) harmonised rules for the placing on the market, the putting into service, and the use of AI systems in the Union; (b) prohibitions of certain AI practices; (c) specific requirements for high-risk AI systems and obligations for operators of such systems; (d) harmonised transparency rules for certain AI systems; (e) harmonised rules for the placing on the market of general-purpose AI models; (f) rules on market monitoring, market surveillance, governance and enforcement; (g) measures to support innovation, with a particular focus on SMEs, including start-ups.
gold_issue_tags: AI Act subject matter; harmonised AI market rules; prohibited AI practices; high-risk AI obligations; AI transparency rules; general-purpose AI model rules; governance and enforcement; SME innovation support
gold_actors: operators of high-risk AI systems; providers of general-purpose AI models; SMEs including start-ups; Union market actors
gold_obligations: the Regulation aims to improve the internal market and promote human-centric trustworthy AI while protecting health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy, rule of law, and environmental protection; the Regulation lays down harmonised rules for placing on the market, putting into service, and use of AI systems in the Union; the Regulation lays down prohibitions of certain AI practices, high-risk AI requirements and operator obligations, transparency rules, general-purpose AI model rules, governance and enforcement rules, and SME-focused innovation measures
gold_support_spans: The purpose of this Regulation is to improve the functioning of the internal market; promote the uptake of human-centric and trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI); ensuring a high level of protection of health, safety, fundamental rights; harmonised rules for the placing on the market, the putting into service, and the use of AI systems in the Union; prohibitions of certain AI practices; specific requirements for high-risk AI systems and obligations for operators of such systems; harmonised transparency rules for certain AI systems; harmonised rules for the placing on the market of general-purpose AI models; rules on market monitoring, market surveillance, governance and enforcement; measures to support innovation, with a particular focus on SMEs, including start-ups
known_confounds: this subject-matter provision states categories rather than detailed operative duties; specific prohibited practices and high-risk requirements are not identified; penalties and timelines are not set in this passage; the passage is limited to AI systems in the Union
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: all AI systems are high-risk; specific compliance steps for a given provider; exact transparency disclosures required; specific SME funding entitlement
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: 4. Non-compliance with any of the following provisions related to operators or notified bodies, other than those laid down in Articles 5, shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 15 000 000 or, if the offender is an undertaking, up to 3 % of its total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year, whichever is higher: (a) obligations of providers pursuant to Article 16; (b) obligations of authorised representatives pursuant to Article 22; (c) obligations of importers pursuant to Article 23; (d) obligations of distributors pursuant to Article 24; (e) obligations of deployers pursuant to Article 26; (f) requirements and obligations of notified bodies pursuant to Article 31, Article 33(1), (3) and (4) or Article 34; (g) transparency obligations for providers and deployers pursuant to Article 50. 5. The supply of incorrect, incomplete or misleading information to notified bodies or national competent authorities in reply to a request shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 7 500 000 or, if the offender is an undertaking, up to 1 % of its total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year, whichever is higher. 6. In the case of SMEs, including start-ups, each fine referred to in this Article shall be up to the percentages or amount referred to in paragraphs 3, 4 and 5, whichever thereof is lower.
gold_issue_tags: administrative fines; operator non-compliance penalties; notified body non-compliance penalties; misleading information penalties; SME fine cap
gold_actors: providers; authorised representatives; importers; distributors; deployers; notified bodies; national competent authorities; SMEs including start-ups
gold_obligations: non-compliance with listed operator or notified body obligations is subject to administrative fines up to EUR 15,000,000 or 3 percent of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher; supplying incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information to notified bodies or national competent authorities is subject to administrative fines up to EUR 7,500,000 or 1 percent of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher; for SMEs including start-ups, each fine is capped at the lower of the relevant percentage or amount
gold_support_spans: shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 15 000 000; up to 3 % of its total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year; obligations of providers pursuant to Article 16; obligations of deployers pursuant to Article 26; transparency obligations for providers and deployers pursuant to Article 50; The supply of incorrect, incomplete or misleading information to notified bodies or national competent authorities; shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 7 500 000; In the case of SMEs, including start-ups, each fine referred to in this Article shall be up to the percentages or amount referred to in paragraphs 3, 4 and 5, whichever thereof is lower

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: 3. By derogation from paragraph 2, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons, including by not materially influencing the outcome of decision making. The first subparagraph shall apply where any of the following conditions is fulfilled: (a) the AI system is intended to perform a narrow procedural task; (b) the AI system is intended to improve the result of a previously completed human activity; (c) the AI system is intended to detect decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment, without proper human review; or (d) the AI system is intended to perform a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III. Notwithstanding the first subparagraph, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall always be considered to be high-risk where the AI system performs profiling of natural persons.
gold_issue_tags: high-risk classification exception; significant risk threshold; decision-making influence; Annex III systems; profiling always high-risk
gold_actors: AI systems referred to in Annex III; natural persons; human reviewers
gold_obligations: an Annex III AI system is not considered high-risk when it does not pose significant risk of harm to health, safety, or fundamental rights, including by not materially influencing decision-making, and one listed condition is fulfilled; listed conditions include narrow procedural tasks, improving a completed human activity, detecting decision-making patterns or deviations without replacing or influencing prior human assessment without proper human review, or preparatory tasks for Annex III assessments; an Annex III AI system is always high-risk where it performs profiling of natural persons
gold_support_spans: shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons; including by not materially influencing the outcome of decision making; intended to perform a narrow procedural task; intended to improve the result of a previously completed human activity; is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment, without proper human review; intended to perform a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III; shall always be considered to be high-risk where the AI system performs profiling of natural persons
known_confounds: conditions apply to Annex III systems and require no significant risk; proper human review is not defined; Annex III use cases are not included; profiling of natural persons overrides the exception
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: all Annex III systems are automatically high-risk without exception; any procedural AI is always exempt; specific risk assessment method or evidence standard; definition of profiling
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: Article 9: Risk management system 1. A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented and maintained in relation to high-risk AI systems. 2. The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system, requiring regular systematic review and updating. It shall comprise the following steps: (a) the identification and analysis of the known and the reasonably foreseeable risks that the high-risk AI system can pose to health, safety or fundamental rights when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose; (b) the estimation and evaluation of the risks that may emerge when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose, and under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse; (c) the evaluation of other risks possibly arising, based on the analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system referred to in Article 72; (d) the adoption of appropriate and targeted risk management measures designed to address the risks identified pursuant to point (a).
gold_issue_tags: risk management system; lifecycle review and updating; risk identification and analysis; foreseeable misuse evaluation; post-market monitoring feedback; targeted risk management measures
gold_actors: responsible risk management actors not specified in excerpt; high-risk AI systems
gold_obligations: a risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented, and maintained for high-risk AI systems; the system shall be a continuous iterative lifecycle process requiring regular systematic review and updating; the system shall identify and analyze known and reasonably foreseeable risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights in intended use; the system shall estimate and evaluate risks under intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; the system shall evaluate risks from post-market monitoring data and adopt appropriate targeted risk management measures
gold_support_spans: shall be established, implemented, documented and maintained in relation to high-risk AI systems; continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system; requiring regular systematic review and updating; identification and analysis of the known and the reasonably foreseeable risks; can pose to health, safety or fundamental rights; under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse; based on the analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system; adoption of appropriate and targeted risk management measures
known_confounds: the actor responsible for establishing the system is not named in this excerpt; specific risk methodology and thresholds are not specified; post-market monitoring system details are outside the passage; examples of risk management measures are not provided
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: one-time risk assessment is sufficient; specific audit frequency; specific acceptable risk threshold; risk management only covers safety and not fundamental rights

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: 6. High-risk AI systems shall be tested for the purpose of identifying the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures. Testing shall ensure that high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose and that they are in compliance with the requirements set out in this Section. 7. Testing procedures may include testing in real-world conditions in accordance with Article 60. 8. The testing of high-risk AI systems shall be performed, as appropriate, at any time throughout the development process, and, in any event, prior to their being placed on the market or put into service. Testing shall be carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds that are appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system. 9. When implementing the risk management system as provided for in paragraphs 1 to 7, providers shall give consideration to whether in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups. 10. For providers of high-risk AI systems that are subject to requirements regarding internal risk management processes under other relevant provisions of Union law, the aspects provided in paragraphs 1 to 9 may be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures established pursuant to that law.
gold_issue_tags: high-risk AI testing; targeted risk management measures; real-world testing option; pre-market testing; defined metrics and thresholds; minor and vulnerable group impact consideration
gold_actors: providers of high-risk AI systems; high-risk AI systems; persons under the age of 18; vulnerable groups
gold_obligations: high-risk AI systems shall be tested to identify appropriate and targeted risk management measures; testing shall ensure intended-purpose consistency and compliance with applicable requirements; testing may include real-world conditions and shall occur as appropriate during development and before market placement or service; testing shall use prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds appropriate to intended purpose; providers shall consider likely adverse impact on persons under 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups; risk management aspects may be combined with other Union-law internal risk management procedures where providers are subject to them
gold_support_spans: High-risk AI systems shall be tested for the purpose of identifying the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures; Testing shall ensure that high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose; in compliance with the requirements set out in this Section; Testing procedures may include testing in real-world conditions; shall be performed, as appropriate, at any time throughout the development process; prior to their being placed on the market or put into service; carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds; providers shall give consideration to whether in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18; and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups; may be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures established pursuant to that law

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: Article 14: Human oversight 1. High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use. 2. Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights that may emerge when a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, in particular where such risks persist despite the application of other requirements set out in this Section. 3. The oversight measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use of the high-risk AI system, and shall be ensured through either one or both of the following types of measures: (a) measures identified and built, when technically feasible, into the high-risk AI system by the provider before it is placed on the market or put into service; (b) measures identified by the provider before placing the high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service and that are appropriate to be implemented by the deployer.
gold_issue_tags: human oversight design; human-machine interface; health safety and fundamental rights risk minimization; proportionate oversight measures; provider and deployer oversight responsibilities
gold_actors: high-risk AI systems; natural persons; providers; deployers
gold_obligations: high-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, so natural persons can effectively oversee them during use; human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimize health, safety, and fundamental rights risks under intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; oversight measures shall be commensurate with risks, autonomy level, and context of use; oversight may be ensured through provider-built measures before market placement or service and provider-identified measures appropriate for deployer implementation
gold_support_spans: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way; with appropriate human-machine interface tools; they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use; Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights; under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse; oversight measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use; measures identified and built, when technically feasible, into the high-risk AI system by the provider; measures identified by the provider before placing the high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service and that are appropriate to be implemented by the deployer
known_confounds: exact oversight interface features are not listed; built-in measures are qualified by technical feasibility; risk commensurateness requires context-specific judgment; the passage does not assign all oversight duties solely to provider or deployer
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: human approval required for every output; manual review eliminates all liability; specific stop-button requirement from this passage alone; oversight may be omitted for autonomous systems
reviewer_notes: Completed by conservative blind review; support spans verified as exact passage substrings.

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: 4. For the purpose of implementing paragraphs 1, 2 and 3, the high-risk AI system shall be provided to the deployer in such a way that natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate: (a) to properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the high-risk AI system and be able to duly monitor its operation, including in view of detecting and addressing anomalies, dysfunctions and unexpected performance; (b) to remain aware of the possible tendency of automatically relying or over-relying on the output produced by a high-risk AI system (automation bias), in particular for high-risk AI systems used to provide information or recommendations for decisions to be taken by natural persons; (c) to correctly interpret the high-risk AI system’s output, taking into account, for example, the interpretation tools and methods available; (d) to decide, in any particular situation, not to use the high-risk AI system or to otherwise disregard, override or reverse the output of the high-risk AI system; (e) to intervene in the operation of the high-risk AI system or interrupt the system through a ‘stop’ button or a similar procedure that allows the system to come to a halt in a safe state.
gold_issue_tags: human oversight capabilities; capacity and limitation understanding; anomaly monitoring; automation bias awareness; output interpretation; override or reverse output; safe interruption
gold_actors: deployers; natural persons assigned human oversight; high-risk AI systems
gold_obligations: high-risk AI systems shall be provided to deployers so assigned oversight persons are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate, to understand capacities and limitations and monitor operations; oversight persons should be enabled to detect and address anomalies, dysfunctions, and unexpected performance; oversight persons should be enabled to remain aware of automation bias and correctly interpret outputs; oversight persons should be enabled to decide not to use the system or to disregard, override, or reverse outputs; oversight persons should be enabled to intervene or interrupt the system through a stop button or similar safe-halt procedure
gold_support_spans: shall be provided to the deployer in such a way that natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned are enabled; to properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the high-risk AI system; be able to duly monitor its operation, including in view of detecting and addressing anomalies, dysfunctions and unexpected performance; to remain aware of the possible tendency of automatically relying or over-relying on the output produced by a high-risk AI system (automation bias); to correctly interpret the high-risk AI system’s output; to decide, in any particular situation, not to use the high-risk AI system or to otherwise disregard, override or reverse the output; to intervene in the operation of the high-risk AI system or interrupt the system through a ‘stop’ button or a similar procedure; allows the system to come to a halt in a safe state
known_confounds: capabilities are qualified by as appropriate and proportionate; the excerpt does not specify who provides training or interface details; stop button or similar procedure may vary by context; automation bias mitigation measures beyond awareness are not defined
unsupported_claims_to_penalize: mandatory physical stop button for every high-risk AI system; oversight persons must be AI experts; deployers may ignore all legal obligations when overriding outputs; automation bias is eliminated by notice alone

source_name: EU AI Act
passage: 7. In the event of the restriction, suspension or withdrawal of a designation, the notifying authority shall: (a) assess the impact on the certificates issued by the notified body; (b) submit a report on its findings to the Commission and the other Member States within three months of having notified the changes to the designation; (c) require the notified body to suspend or withdraw, within a reasonable period of time determined by the authority, any certificates which were unduly issued, in order to ensure the continuing conformity of high-risk AI systems on the market; (d) inform the Commission and the Member States about certificates the suspension or withdrawal of which it has required; (e) provide the national competent authorities of the Member State in which the provider has its registered place of business with all relevant information about the certificates of which it has required the suspension or withdrawal; that authority shall take the appropriate measures, where necessary, to avoid a potential risk to health, safety or fundamental rights.
gold_issue_tags: notified body designation restriction; certificate impact assessment; reporting to Commission and Member States; unduly issued certificate suspension or withdrawal; competent authority information sharing; risk mitigation
gold_actors: notifying authority; notified body; Commission; Member States; national competent authorities; providers
gold_obligations: when a designation is restricted, suspended, or withdrawn, the notifying authority shall assess certificate impacts; the notifying authority shall report findings to the Commission and other Member States within three months of notifying the designation changes; the notifying authority shall require the notified body to suspend or withdraw unduly issued certificates within a reasonable period set by the authority; the notifying authority shall inform the Commission and Member States about required certificate suspensions or withdrawals and provide relevant information to national competent authorities where the provider is registered; that competent authority shall take appropriate measures where necessary to avoid potential risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights
